# gRPC Tunnel Design (/docs/architecture/grpc-tunnel)



gRPC Tunnel Design [#grpc-tunnel-design]

The current transport uses one long-lived gRPC stream per active tunnel.

Benefits:

* outbound-only client connectivity
* low-latency server-to-client request dispatch
* room for heartbeats and future stream extensions
* simpler operator deployment than request polling

Current limitations:

* WebSocket support is still pending
* tunnel metadata is not yet fully server-owned


# Architecture / Spec (/docs/architecture)



Architecture / Spec [#architecture--spec]

The current implementation uses:

* public HTTP ingress on the server
* a gRPC tunnel stream from client to server
* request forwarding over that stream
* server-side admin APIs and UI

Architecture Pages [#architecture-pages]

* [Traffic Flow](/docs/architecture/traffic-flow)
* [gRPC Tunnel Design](/docs/architecture/grpc-tunnel)
* [Rewrite Direction](/docs/architecture/rewrite-direction)


# Rewrite Direction (/docs/architecture/rewrite-direction)



Rewrite Direction [#rewrite-direction]

The rewrite target is a stronger tunnel platform with:

* server-owned tunnel records
* OIDC-based human authentication
* separate agent credentials for machine tunnel registration
* better observability and request logs
* access controls at ingress
* WebSocket support

The current codebase now includes the first persistence foundation:

* SQLite-backed tunnel records
* persisted request logs
* stronger client runtime-state cleanup


# Traffic Flow (/docs/architecture/traffic-flow)



Traffic Flow [#traffic-flow]

Current flow:

1. Client opens a gRPC stream to the server.
2. Client registers a hostname.
3. Browser sends a request to `https://<hostname>`.
4. Server looks up the active tunnel.
5. Server sends a proxied request over the stream.
6. Client forwards that request to the local app.
7. Client returns the response over the stream.
8. Server returns that response to the browser.

This model works behind NAT because the client initiates the outbound connection.


# CLI Device Flow (/docs/authentication/device-flow)



CLI Device Flow [#cli-device-flow]

Log in from the CLI:

```bash
export FWDX_SERVER=https://tunnel.example.com
fwdx login
```

The CLI prints:

* verification URL
* user code
* direct verification URL when the provider exposes one

After approval:

```bash
fwdx whoami
```

Management commands reuse the stored session:

```bash
fwdx manage tunnels --server https://tunnel.example.com
fwdx domains add my.domain --server https://tunnel.example.com
```

The stored session lives at:

```text
~/.fwdx/auth.json
```


# Authentication (/docs/authentication)



Authentication [#authentication]

`fwdx` now has a split auth model:

* humans authenticate with OIDC
* tunnel runtimes authenticate with per-agent credentials

Human auth [#human-auth]

* Admin UI: OIDC authorization code flow with PKCE
* CLI: OIDC device authorization flow via `fwdx login`

Tunnel runtime auth [#tunnel-runtime-auth]

Tunnel clients do not use a shared global runtime token anymore.

* the server issues a credential to a named agent
* tunnel definitions are stored on the server and assigned to an agent
* the local client stores its assigned agent credential in `~/.fwdx/client.json`

Pages [#pages]

* [OIDC UI Login](/docs/authentication/oidc-ui)
* [CLI Device Flow](/docs/authentication/device-flow)


# OIDC UI Login (/docs/authentication/oidc-ui)



OIDC UI Login [#oidc-ui-login]

Required server settings:

```bash
export FWDX_OIDC_ISSUER=https://issuer.example.com
export FWDX_OIDC_CLIENT_ID=fwdx-web
export FWDX_OIDC_CLIENT_SECRET=your-secret
export FWDX_OIDC_REDIRECT_URL=https://tunnel.example.com/auth/oidc/callback
export FWDX_OIDC_ADMIN_EMAILS=admin@example.com
```

The redirect URL must point back to the `fwdx` server:

```text
https://tunnel.example.com/auth/oidc/callback
```

Once configured, visit:

```text
https://tunnel.example.com/admin/ui
```

The UI redirects to the OIDC provider and returns with a session cookie issued by `fwdx`.


# CLI Usage (/docs/cli)



CLI Usage [#cli-usage]

The current CLI has four areas:

* server startup
* OIDC login
* admin management
* tunnel lifecycle

Server [#server]

```bash
fwdx serve \
  --hostname tunnel.example.com \
  --oidc-issuer https://issuer.example.com \
  --oidc-client-id fwdx-web \
  --oidc-client-secret ... \
  --oidc-redirect-url https://tunnel.example.com/auth/oidc/callback
```

Human auth [#human-auth]

```bash
fwdx login
fwdx whoami
fwdx logout
```

Management [#management]

```bash
fwdx manage tunnels --server https://tunnel.example.com
fwdx manage domains list --server https://tunnel.example.com
fwdx domains add my.domain --server https://tunnel.example.com
```

Tunnel runtime [#tunnel-runtime]

```bash
export FWDX_SERVER=https://tunnel.example.com

fwdx tunnel create -l localhost:3000 -s app --name app
fwdx tunnel start app
fwdx tunnel stop app
fwdx tunnel list
fwdx logs app --follow
```

The CLI provisions an agent credential automatically on first tunnel create/start and stores it locally.


# Deployment (/docs/deployment)



Deployment [#deployment]

The recommended deployment model is:

1. `fwdx` server running locally on private ports
2. nginx terminating TLS on `443` and `4443`
3. wildcard DNS for `*.tunnel.example.com`

Sections [#sections]

* [nginx](/docs/deployment/nginx)
* [systemd](/docs/deployment/systemd)
* [TLS and DNS](/docs/deployment/tls-dns)


# nginx (/docs/deployment/nginx)



nginx [#nginx]

The common layout is:

* `443` -> fwdx web port
* `4443` -> fwdx gRPC port

If you use ingress IP allowlists, start the server with trusted proxy CIDRs so `fwdx` uses the real visitor IP instead of nginx's loopback address:

```bash
fwdx serve \
  --trusted-proxy-cidrs 127.0.0.1/32,::1/128 \
  ...
```

HTTPS vhost [#https-vhost]

```nginx
server {
  listen 443 ssl http2;
  listen [::]:443 ssl http2;
  server_name tunnel.example.com *.tunnel.example.com;

  ssl_certificate     /etc/letsencrypt/live/tunnel.example.com/fullchain.pem;
  ssl_certificate_key /etc/letsencrypt/live/tunnel.example.com/privkey.pem;

  location / {
    proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:4040;
    proxy_http_version 1.1;
    proxy_set_header Host $host;
    proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
    proxy_read_timeout 86400s;
    proxy_send_timeout 86400s;
  }
}
```

gRPC stream block [#grpc-stream-block]

```nginx
stream {
  server {
    listen 4443 ssl;
    listen [::]:4443 ssl;

    ssl_certificate     /etc/letsencrypt/live/tunnel.example.com/fullchain.pem;
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/letsencrypt/live/tunnel.example.com/privkey.pem;
    ssl_alpn h2;

    proxy_pass 127.0.0.1:4440;
    proxy_connect_timeout 10s;
    proxy_timeout 86400s;
  }
}
```


# systemd (/docs/deployment/systemd)



systemd [#systemd]

Example service:

```ini
[Unit]
Description=fwdx server
After=network.target nginx.service

[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/fwdx serve \
  --hostname tunnel.example.com \
  --oidc-issuer https://issuer.example.com \
  --oidc-client-id fwdx-web \
  --oidc-client-secret YOUR_OIDC_SECRET \
  --oidc-redirect-url https://tunnel.example.com/auth/oidc/callback \
  --oidc-admin-emails admin@example.com \
  --web-port 4040 \
  --grpc-port 4440 \
  --data-dir /var/lib/fwdx
Restart=always
RestartSec=5

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

Logs:

```bash
journalctl -u fwdx -f
```


# TLS and DNS (/docs/deployment/tls-dns)



TLS and DNS [#tls-and-dns]

DNS [#dns]

You need:

* `tunnel.example.com` -> server public IP
* `*.tunnel.example.com` -> server public IP

TLS [#tls]

The certificate must cover:

* `tunnel.example.com`
* `*.tunnel.example.com`

If nginx terminates TLS, fwdx itself can stay on plain local ports.


# fwdx Docs (/docs)



fwdx [#fwdx]

`fwdx` is a self-hosted tunnel platform for exposing local HTTP services through your own domain.

Documentation [#documentation]

* [Installation](/docs/installation)
* [CLI Usage](/docs/cli)
* [Deployment](/docs/deployment)
* [Authentication](/docs/authentication)
* [Architecture / Spec](/docs/architecture)

Current Product Scope [#current-product-scope]

* gRPC-based tunnel transport
* Nginx-friendly deployment model
* Admin UI and management APIs
* Detached client tunnel runtime
* Tunnel request stats and persisted request logs

Next Rewrite Direction [#next-rewrite-direction]

The platform is being moved toward:

* server-owned control-plane state
* OIDC-based human authentication
* separate machine credentials for client agents
* stronger tunnel observability
* WebSocket support


# Client Install (/docs/installation/client)



Client Install [#client-install]

Install or build the same `fwdx` binary on the client machine.

Set client configuration:

```bash
export FWDX_SERVER=https://tunnel.example.com
export FWDX_TUNNEL_PORT=4443
```

Create and start a tunnel:

```bash
fwdx tunnel create -l localhost:5173 -s vite --name vite
fwdx tunnel start vite
```

The first tunnel create/start on a machine provisions and stores a local agent credential in `~/.fwdx/client.json`.

Detached mode:

```bash
fwdx tunnel start vite --detach
fwdx logs vite --follow
```


# Installation (/docs/installation)



Installation [#installation]

`fwdx` has two sides:

* a public server
* one or more tunnel clients

Requirements [#requirements]

* Go `1.25+` to build from source
* a Linux server with a public IP for the server side
* a domain such as `tunnel.example.com`
* TLS certificates for the public hostname

Paths [#paths]

* Server binary: `/usr/local/bin/fwdx`
* Client config: `~/.fwdx/client.json`
* Detached runtime state: `~/.fwdx/run`

Next Steps [#next-steps]

* [Install the server](/docs/installation/server)
* [Install the client](/docs/installation/client)


# Server Install (/docs/installation/server)



Server Install [#server-install]

Build from source:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/BRAVO68WEB/fwdx.git
cd fwdx
go build -o fwdx .
sudo mv fwdx /usr/local/bin/
```

Run behind nginx:

```bash
fwdx serve \
  --hostname tunnel.example.com \
  --oidc-issuer https://issuer.example.com \
  --oidc-client-id fwdx-web \
  --oidc-client-secret YOUR_OIDC_SECRET \
  --oidc-redirect-url https://tunnel.example.com/auth/oidc/callback \
  --oidc-admin-emails admin@example.com \
  --web-port 4040 \
  --grpc-port 4440 \
  --data-dir /var/lib/fwdx
```

The web port serves proxy traffic, admin UI, OIDC callback routes, and admin APIs. The gRPC port serves tunnel clients.
